unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! This unit is all about actions and activities — the things people DO. You will learn lots of action words (verbs) such as build, discuss, protect and celebrate. You will talk about things you did, using the past simple, and learn to explain why things happened using words like because and so. You will put events in order with First, Next, Then and Finally, ask Wh- questions, and share your ideas politely. By the end, you will be ready to read this unit's story, “The Wall Behind the Garden”, where a class of children work together to build something special.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few action words each day.
Do the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one or two at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Wall Behind the Garden”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children using action words during active learning

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To talk about something with other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will discuss our ideas in a group.
practiceSentences: We will discuss our ideas in a group.
Partners discuss their answers before telling the class.
Shall we discuss which game to play at break?
Yusuf said, "Let us discuss the ending of the story."
I discuss my drawings with my big sister.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - c - u - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'discuss', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your ears to hear carefully.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
practiceSentences: Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
We listen quietly when the imam gives a talk.
Do you listen for the birds in the morning?
The coach said, "Listen for the whistle, then run."
I listen carefully when Dad explains new things.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - s - t - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'listen', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say kindly that you will give or do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will offer help if my friend falls down.
practiceSentences: I will offer help if my friend falls down.
Neighbours offer food to each other during Ramadan.
Will you offer your turn to the younger boy?
Musa said, "I offer to sweep the classroom on Mondays."
I offer my umbrella when a friend gets wet.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'offer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something by putting parts together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will build a wall with smooth stones.
practiceSentences: We will build a wall with smooth stones.
The workers build the new classroom brick by brick.
Can we build a bridge with these blocks?
Samira said, "Birds build their nests with twigs and grass."
I helped Grandpa build a small wooden shelf.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'build', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To finish something or make it whole.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will complete my homework before dinner.
practiceSentences: I will complete my homework before dinner.
Please complete the puzzle before starting a new one.
Did you complete your reading chart this week?
The teacher said, "Complete the sentences with neat handwriting."
I feel proud when I complete a hard task.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - p - l - e - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'complete', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To take something away from a place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please remove your shoes before you come in.
practiceSentences: Please remove your shoes before you come in.
Please remove the muddy boots at the door.
Can you remove the stickers without tearing the page?
Mum said, "Remove your coat and hang it up."
I remove my shoes before entering the mosque.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - m - o - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'remove', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To grow or become better over time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We develop our reading by practising every day.
practiceSentences: We develop our reading by practising every day.
Strong readers develop their skills by practising daily.
How do seeds develop into tall sunflowers?
The coach said, "Practice helps your muscles develop."
I develop my handwriting by copying neat letters.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - v - e - l - o - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'develop', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To keep someone or something safe from harm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A helmet helps protect your head.
practiceSentences: A helmet helps protect your head.
Thick gloves protect your hands from the thorns.
How do umbrellas protect us from the rain?
Dad said, "Seatbelts protect everyone in the car."
I protect my little sister when we cross roads.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - t - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'protect', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go from one place to another, often far away.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will travel to the village by bus.
practiceSentences: We will travel to the village by bus.
Families travel by bus to the Eid fair.
How long does it take to travel to the coast?
Ishmael said, "One day I will travel by ship."
I travel to school on foot with my brother.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - a - v - e - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'travel', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To get away from a place or a situation.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The goat tried to escape from the pen.
practiceSentences: The goat tried to escape from the pen.
The clever cat tried to escape through the window.
How did the chickens escape from the coop?
Yusuf said, "The rabbit dug a hole to escape."
I read about a fish that could escape every net.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - s - c - a - p - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'escape', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Let us search for my lost pencil.
practiceSentences: Let us search for my lost pencil.
The children search the shelf for the missing globe.
Shall we search under the desks for your pencil?
Aisha said, "Search your bag again before you worry."
I search the sky for the new moon before Eid.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - a - r - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'search', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To do something happy and special for a good time or a win.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will celebrate after the spelling contest.
practiceSentences: We will celebrate after the spelling contest.
Our class will celebrate finishing one hundred books.
How does your family celebrate Eid morning?
Musa said, "We celebrate with dates and sweet tea."
I celebrate every goal my team scores.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - e - l - e - b - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'celebrate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: What will happen in the story next?
practiceSentences: What will happen in the story next?
Exciting things happen on the last day of term.
What will happen if we mix blue and yellow?
Samira said, "Funny things always happen at Grandma's house."
I wonder what will happen in chapter three.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - a - p - p - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'happen', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Actions and Activities.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and use many action words, such as build, listen, protect and celebrate.
Talk about things you did, using the past simple (built, listened, celebrated).
Explain why something happened, using because and so.
Put events in order, using First, Next, Then, Finally.
Ask questions with what, where, who, when, why and how.
Share your ideas politely, using I think and I don't think.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and understand a story about working together to solve a problem.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: A Busy Saturday
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: On Saturday, our family had a busy day. First, we cleaned the yard together. My brother removed the old sticks, and I helped build a small stone path. Then we searched the garden for ripe tomatoes. Finally, we celebrated our hard work with sweet tea and laughing. It was a wonderful day!

readings.title: Helping Hands
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Samira saw her neighbour carrying a heavy basket. “Can I offer some help?” she asked politely. Together they carried the basket home. The neighbour smiled. “Thank you! You have a kind heart.” Samira felt happy because she had helped someone.

readings.title: Planning the Garden
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “First, we will search for good seeds. Then, we will build a small fence to protect the plants. After that, we will water the garden every day. Finally, we will celebrate when the first flower grows!”

readings.title: What Happened?
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Yesterday the wind blew hard and the wall began to shake. We ran to hold the frame. We did not complete the support, so we will fix it today. I think we should add more stones.”

readings.title: The Wall Behind the Garden
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was the first week of school in a small village. The rain had stopped, and the fields were dry and warm. The children helped sweep the schoolyard and water the garden behind the classrooms.
That morning, the teacher smiled and said, “Today we will start a special project. Together, we will build a new reading wall in the garden.”
“What will be on the wall?” asked Nadia.
“Stories, poems, and pictures,” said the teacher. “But first, we must remove the old, broken fence.”
The class went outside. Some children pulled weeds. Others removed old wires and sticks. Then they saw something strange — a hole in the ground, covered by stones and dry leaves.
“What happened here?” asked Noah, looking closely.
“I don't know,” said Nadia. “Let's discuss it with the teacher.”
The teacher looked at the hole and said, “A long time ago, this was an animal path. Goats used to escape from this yard right here.”
The children laughed. “We don't want goats in our classroom!”
“Then we must block the path well,” said the teacher. “Let's plan carefully and build a strong wall.”
First, they searched for smooth, flat stones. Then, they discussed the design and the colours. Finally, they began to build.
Nadia painted birds. Noah drew a blue waterfall. Some children wrote short poems.
But suddenly the clouds turned dark. The wind blew hard, and the wall began to shake.
“Hold the frame!” shouted the teacher. Nadia ran to help. Together they held the pieces until the wind slowed down.
“We need to fix it,” said Noah. “We didn't complete the support.”
“I think we should add more stones,” said Nadia.
“I don't think paint alone is strong enough to hold it,” said another child.
The next day, everyone came early. “We will finish today,” said the teacher. “Let's be strong and work together.” They completed the wall and made it much stronger.
The final touch was a sign that read: “We built this wall to share words and protect dreams.”
Later that week, the whole village came to celebrate. There was singing, clapping, and food from every family.
“This is a true celebration,” said the teacher. “You all helped. You worked hard.”
“Nadia's drawing is my favourite,” said the principal.
“And Noah's poem,” said the teacher. “He wrote about how we protect our stories and our voices.”
After the event, Nadia saw a younger girl standing near the wall.
“Can I draw here one day?” the girl asked shyly.
“Of course,” said Nadia kindly. “This wall is for everyone.”
That night, Nadia wrote in her notebook: “We built something beautiful. It was windy and messy. But we listened to each other. We shared our ideas. We offered help. And we finished something strong.”
The next morning, a visitor came to the school. He had travelled from a nearby town.
“I work in education,” he said. “I heard you made something special.” The students proudly gave him a tour of the wall.
“You are helping this place develop,” he said. “This wall is not just a wall. It is a gift.”
When he left, Noah asked, “Why did he call it a gift?”
“Because it gives people ideas,” said the teacher. “And new ideas are part of how a village grows and develops.”
Nadia nodded and smiled. “And we made it,” she said. “Together.”

grammar.title: The Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about actions that already happened and are finished. It helps us tell stories and share things we did. In this unit's story, the children built a wall — that already happened, so we say built, not build.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For most verbs, add -ed:  discuss → discussed, listen → listened, celebrate → celebrated.
Some verbs are special (irregular). You must learn these:  build → built,  go → went,  have → had.
Often we add a time word:  yesterday, last week, a long time ago.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yesterday they build a house.”  ✗
Fix: The action is finished, so use the past form: “Yesterday they built a house.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The past simple is a little time machine. If it is done, take it back in time — add -ed for most verbs, and remember the special ones like built and went.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets: | Last summer they ______ (build) a reading wall. | She ______ (celebrate) her birthday with friends. | He ______ (remove) the old sign yesterday. | We ______ (listen) to the teacher this morning.

grammar.title: Sentence Starters for Explanations
grammar.explanation: Sometimes we need to explain why something happened or when it happened. Special starter words like because, when, since and as help us do this. They make our sentences clearer and more interesting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
because → gives the reason:  “I escaped because I was scared.”
when → tells the time:  “It happened when it was dark.”
since → gives a reason:  “She was happy since she got good news.”
as → time or reason:  “They laughed as they watched the show.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I left because.”  ✗  (The thought is not finished.)
Fix: Always finish the idea: “I left because I was tired.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A starter word is a door — but you must always walk through it. After because, always say the rest of the reason.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with because, when, since or as: | I ran fast ______ I wanted to catch the bus. | We stayed inside ______ it was too cold. | He was quiet ______ it was a serious moment. | It happened ______ it was dark outside.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions! Wh- questions begin with what, where, who, when, why and how. They help us ask for real information, not just a yes or no answer.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What happened?  (asking about a thing or event)
Where did you travel?  (asking about a place — note the helping word did)
Who offered help?  (asking about a person)
When did you finish?  ·  Why did the wall shake?  ·  How did you fix it?
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Where you traveled?”  ✗  (missing the helping word)
Fix: In past questions, use did and the base verb: “Where did you travel?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Wh- words want answers! The Wh- word always comes first, at the front of the question.
grammar.practice: Complete each question with the correct Wh- word: | ______ is your favourite colour? | ______ did you go last weekend? | ______ helped you with your project? | ______ did the wall shake? (ask for a reason)

grammar.title: Cause and Effect (because / so)
grammar.explanation: We often want to show why something happens (the cause) and what happens next (the effect). Because gives the reason. So shows the result.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
because → reason:  “He removed the trash because it smelled.”
so → result:  “She offered help, so I listened.”
Put a comma before so when it joins two full ideas.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She was happy so she smiled.”  ✗  (no comma)
Fix: “She was happy, so she smiled.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Because explains the reason. So shows what happens. “Because explains, So shows!”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with because or so: | The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones. | He was late ______ he missed the bus. | The flowers wilted ______ they did not get water. | I forgot my homework, ______ I felt sad.

grammar.title: Time Words for Sequencing
grammar.explanation: Time words tell us the order of events — what came first, next and last. They help us tell a clear story, just like the children who planned and built the wall step by step.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
First, they searched for smooth stones.
Then, they discussed the design and colours.
Next, they began to build.
Finally, they celebrated together.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They started to build. First, they discussed the idea.”  ✗  (wrong order)
Fix: Put the events in the right order: “First, they discussed the idea. Then, they started to build.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Time words are a little story map: First → Next → Then → Finally. Follow the map and no one gets lost.
grammar.practice: Put the time words in order. Fill in with First, Next, Then or Finally: | ______, they gathered their materials. | ______, they discussed the plan. | ______, they built the wall. | ______, they celebrated their success!

grammar.title: Polite Opinions + Word Families
grammar.explanation: When we share what we think — especially if we disagree — it is kind to say it gently. Polite opinion phrases help us share ideas in a friendly, respectful way.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
I think …  →  “I think traveling is fun.”
I don't think …  →  “I don't think escaping was the best idea.”
In my opinion …  or  I believe …  →  “In my opinion, reading is important.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Traveling is boring!”  ✗  (sounds strong and unkind)
Fix: Soften it: “I think traveling can be boring sometimes.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A polite opinion is like a soft pillow. Adding I think makes your idea comfortable for everyone to hear.
grammar.practice: Rewrite each strong sentence as a polite opinion using I think or I don't think: | Group work is fun. | Staying up late is a good idea. | This story is exciting. | Lesson 7:  Word Families | What it means and why it matters | Word families are groups of words that grow from the same base word. If you learn one word, you can unlock several others! This makes your vocabulary much bigger. | How to use it | Add -er for the person who does it:  build → builder. | Add -ment for the result:  develop → development. | Add -tion for the process:  protect → protection;  celebrate → celebration. | A common mistake | Mistake: “I want to buildment a sandcastle.”  ✗ | Fix: Use the base word for the action: “I want to build a sandcastle.”  ✓ | Memory tip | A word family is a tree. The base word is the trunk, and each ending (-er, -ment, -tion) is a branch that grows its own meaning. | Practice 7 | Write the correct form of the word in brackets: | The ______ (build) is making a new house. | The ______ (develop) of the garden took many weeks. | A helmet gives good ______ (protect).

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Tell What You Did
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think of something you did yesterday or last week. Get ready to say three sentences in order:
“First, I ______.”
“Then, I ______.”
“Finally, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use the past simple? Did you use time words? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise tell what you did and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Share a Polite Opinion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a topic (a game, a food, a story). Say what you think, politely:
“I think ______ because ______.”
“I don't think ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise share a polite opinion and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — What Happened Next?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person starts a story: “The goat tried to escape from the pen …”
The next person adds one action: “Then it ran to the garden and ______.”
Keep taking turns, using action words, until the story is finished. Ask “What happened next?” each time.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise what happened next? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — How To Do Something
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four steps to do a simple activity, such as how to plant a seed or how to tidy your room. Use time words:
First, ______.
Next, ______.
Then, ______.
Finally, ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about how to do something.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Something You Built or Made
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three or four sentences about a time you built, made, or fixed something. Answer these questions in your writing:
What did you build or make?
Who helped you?
What happened while you were doing it?
How did you feel when you completed it?
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about something you built or made.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Match the Action Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each action word to what it means. Write the letter next to the number.
protect      (a) to look for something
search       (b) to finish something
complete   (c) to keep something safe
celebrate   (d) to do something happy for a special time
Answers: 1-c   2-a   3-b   4-d
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Past Simple
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday we ______ (build) a stone path.
She ______ (celebrate) her birthday last week.
The children ______ (listen) to the teacher.
He ______ (remove) the broken fence.
Answers: 1. built   2. celebrated   3. listened   4. removed
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — because or so?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with because or so.
The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones.
I wore a helmet ______ I wanted to protect my head.
It started to rain, ______ we went inside.
She smiled ______ she was happy.
Answers: 1. so   2. because   3. so   4. because
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Put the Steps in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems:
These sentences about building the wall are mixed up. Write them in the correct order using First, Then, Next, Finally.
They celebrated together.
They searched for smooth stones.
They discussed the design.
They began to build.
Answer: First, they searched for smooth stones. Then, they discussed the design. Next, they began to build. Finally, they celebrated together.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Actions and Activities learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — How To Do Something and a recording of Speaking 1 — Tell What You Did. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'discuss' mean?
quizzes.options: To finish something or make it whole. | To make something by putting parts together. | To say kindly that you will give or do something. | To talk about something with other people.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To talk about something with other people.
quizzes.explanation: We will discuss our ideas in a group.

quizzes.question: What does 'listen' mean?
quizzes.options: To finish something or make it whole. | To make something by putting parts together. | To take something away from a place. | To use your ears to hear carefully.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To use your ears to hear carefully.
quizzes.explanation: Please listen to the teacher when she talks.

quizzes.question: What does 'offer' mean?
quizzes.options: To finish something or make it whole. | To grow or become better over time. | To say kindly that you will give or do something. | To take something away from a place.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To say kindly that you will give or do something.
quizzes.explanation: I will offer help if my friend falls down.

quizzes.question: What does 'build' mean?
quizzes.options: To grow or become better over time. | To keep someone or something safe from harm. | To make something by putting parts together. | To take something away from a place.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make something by putting parts together.
quizzes.explanation: We will build a wall with smooth stones.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Wall Behind the Garden': “Let's _____ it with the teacher.” The teacher looked at the hole and said, “A long time ago, this was an animal path.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Let's discuss it with the teacher.” The teacher looked at the hole and said, “A long time ago, this was an animal path.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Helping Hands': “Can I _____ some help?” she asked politely.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Can I offer some help?” she asked politely.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Busy Saturday': My brother removed the old sticks, and I helped _____ a small stone path.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: My brother removed the old sticks, and I helped build a small stone path.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'What Happened?': We did not _____ the support, so we will fix it today.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: We did not complete the support, so we will fix it today.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Wall Behind the Garden': “But first, we must _____ the old, broken fence.” The class went outside.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “But first, we must remove the old, broken fence.” The class went outside.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Wall Behind the Garden': “You are helping this place _____,” he said.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “You are helping this place develop,” he said.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the ‘Planning the Garden’ text with me and explain it?”
“Can you play a listening game where you tell a plan and I put the steps in order?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise telling what I did yesterday?”
“Can you play ‘What happened next?’ with me?”
“Can you check if my opinion sounds polite?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if I used the past simple correctly?”
“Can you help me put my steps in the right order?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these four action words?”
“Can you give me another sentence using the word offer?”
“What is the difference between listen and hear?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my sentence with the word protect?”
“Can you show me the word families for build, develop and protect?”
“Can you give me more practice with complete and remove?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you play a hiding game where I search for a word?”
“Can you help me say a sentence about a place I want to travel to?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you ask me a ‘what will happen next?’ question?”
“How do I say celebrate in the past — celebrated?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you show me more word families from Unit 5?”
“When do I use protect and when do I use protection?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘they built’ but ‘they build’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise because and so?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To talk about something with other people. We will discuss our ideas in a group.
To use your ears to hear carefully. Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
To say kindly that you will give or do something. I will offer help if my friend falls down.
To make something by putting parts together. We will build a wall with smooth stones.
To finish something or make it whole. I will complete my homework before dinner.
To take something away from a place. Please remove your shoes before you come in.
To grow or become better over time. We develop our reading by practising every day.
To keep someone or something safe from harm. A helmet helps protect your head.
To go from one place to another, often far away. We will travel to the village by bus.
To get away from a place or a situation. The goat tried to escape from the pen.
